sound_type: higher-pitched cackling call -- rapid 'cack-cack-cack' or 'yip-yip' at 400-2000 Hz, higher and thinner than Canada Goose, flock-driven chattering texture, E=4 flock-amplified, smaller body produces higher pitch
ecosystem: Field/lake, winter visitor. Agricultural fields, lakeshores, and grassy areas in PNW lowlands during winter. Breeds on Arctic and sub-Arctic tundra. Purely migratory -- no resident populations in PNW.
season: Winter visitor (November-March). Fall migration peaks October-November. Spring migration peaks March-April. Breeds on Arctic tundra in summer.

coordinates.description: Reference for Willamette Valley, Oregon -- primary wintering ground for B. h. minima (smallest subspecies). Tens of thousands of Cackling Geese in agricultural fields.

taxonomy_note.description: THIRTEENTH consecutive Anatidae (118-130). Split from Canada Goose in 2004 by AOU. Completes goose comparison pair (129-130). Second Branta genus entry. E=4 through flock-amplified higher-pitched cackling. Purely migratory -- Arctic tundra breeder. Four subspecies including recovered Aleutian Cackling Goose.
taxonomy_note.current_family: Anatidae (13th consecutive -- RECORD EXTENDS, streak ongoing)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Branta from Old Norse brandgas ('burnt goose'). hutchinsii honors Thomas Hutchins, 18th-century naturalist who described the small tundra geese of Hudson Bay.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Branta -- second entry. Canada Goose (129) and Cackling Goose (130). Both Branta. Genus continues at 131 (Brant).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anseriformes. Thirteenth consecutive Anatidae. The goose era continues.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae at thirteenth consecutive. The goose comparison pair (129-130) is complete. Fifth comparison pair in SPS.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 400-2,000 Hz. Higher fundamental than Canada Goose (200-2,500 Hz). Energy centered in 600-1,200 Hz band.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 600-1,200 Hz. Higher than Canada Goose's 300-1,000 Hz peak. The squeaky quality comes from this higher center.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Cackle: 400-2,000 Hz. Alarm: sharper, more rapid. Flight call: slightly higher than ground call.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Rapid: individual calls 0.2-0.5 seconds. Given in quick series. Faster call rate than Canada Goose.
song_metrics.note_structure: HIGHER CACKLE: Rapid 'cack-cack-cack' or 'yip-yip.' Higher-pitched, thinner, squeakier than Canada Goose's two-syllable 'ah-HONK.' Smaller syrinx produces higher pitch.
song_metrics.note_types: Contact cackle (primary, flock communication), alarm call (sharper, more rapid), flight call (slightly higher), feeding chatter (continuous low cackling).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Rapid series of cackles. Faster repetition rate than Canada Goose. Asynchronous flock cackling creates chattering texture rather than rolling honk wall.
song_metrics.timbre: Higher. Thinner. Squeaky. Less resonant than Canada Goose. More rapid. The 'frantic' quality distinguishes it from the Canada Goose's deeper authority.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Individual call is less powerful than Canada Goose. Flock-amplified. Thousands in agricultural fields produce substantial collective sound. One step below Canada Goose's E=5.
song_metrics.tempo: Fast. Rapid cackling with short gaps between calls. Faster tempo than Canada Goose.
song_metrics.context: Flock communication, flight coordination, feeding, alarm. Same multi-purpose function as Canada Goose but at higher pitch and faster tempo.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Varies by subspecies. B. h. minima (smallest) produces highest pitch. Size correlates with pitch across all four subspecies.
song_metrics.key_property: HIGHER-PITCHED CACKLING -- E=4 FLOCK-AMPLIFIED. Rapid 'cack-cack-cack' at 400-2,000 Hz. Higher, thinner, squeakier than Canada Goose. THIRTEENTH CONSECUTIVE ANATIDAE (118-130). GOOSE COMPARISON PAIR COMPLETE (129-130). Split from Canada Goose in 2004. Purely migratory. Arctic tundra breeder. Aleutian subspecies: near-extinction to recovery. Willamette Valley primary PNW winter site.

plumage.overall: Similar to Canada Goose: black head and neck with white cheek patch. Brown-gray body. Smaller overall. Some subspecies have darker breast than Canada Goose.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Smaller size. Stubbier, shorter bill. Shorter neck. Rounder, steeper forehead. White cheek patch (smaller than Canada Goose in some subspecies). Size is the primary separator.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Adults similar year-round. Juveniles slightly duller.
plumage.flight_pattern: Flock formation. Faster wingbeats than Canada Goose (smaller body). Higher-pitched flight calls. Looks more compact in flight.

physical_description.overall: Body length 56-64 cm (22-25 inches). Weight 0.9-1.8 kg (2-4 lbs). Wingspan 110-120 cm. Notably smaller than Canada Goose in all dimensions.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Small size. Stubby bill. Short neck. Steep forehead. In mixed flocks with Canada Goose, the size difference is obvious.
physical_description.field_id: In mixed flocks: look for the noticeably smaller birds with stubbier bills and shorter necks. Listen for the higher-pitched cackle among the deeper honks. Voice is often the fastest separator.

nodes.title: Higher-Pitched Cackling Call -- E=4 Flock-Amplified
nodes.description: Rapid 'cack-cack-cack' at 400-2,000 Hz. Higher, thinner, squeakier than Canada Goose. Flock chattering texture. Voice as fastest field separator. Smaller body = higher pitch.

nodes.title: The 2004 AOU Species Split
nodes.description: Four smallest subspecies elevated from Canada Goose to Branta hutchinsii. Molecular genetics + bimodal size distribution + different breeding ecology. One of the most significant recent North American taxonomic decisions.

nodes.title: Arctic Tundra Breeder -- Purely Migratory
nodes.description: Breeds on Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta, Aleutian Islands, Arctic Canada. Purely migratory -- no PNW resident populations. Short Arctic breeding season. Aleutian subspecies recovered from near-extinction. Conservation parallel to Trumpeter Swan (128).

nodes.title: Goose Comparison Pair Complete (129-130)
nodes.description: Canada Goose (129, large, deep honk, E=5, resident) vs. Cackling Goose (130, small, higher cackle, E=4, winter visitor). Fifth comparison pair in SPS. Mirrors Hairy/Downy WP (110-111), Barrow's/Common Goldeneye (123-124), Tundra/Trumpeter Swan (127-128).

nodes.title: Thirteenth Consecutive Anatidae (118-130) -- Record Extends
nodes.description: Post-climax energy descent: E=6 (128), E=5 (129), E=4 (130). Goose era second entry. Second Branta genus. Ducks (118-126), swans (127-128), geese (129+). All three PNW Anatidae subfamilies.

cross_references.relationship: GOOSE PAIR COMPLETE! Canada (129, E=5, large, deep honk, resident) vs. Cackling (130, E=4, small, higher cackle, winter visitor). Fifth comparison pair in SPS. Split in 2004.
GOOSE ERA CONTINUES! Cackling (130, E=4, field) to Brant (131, E=4, marine). Both small Branta geese at E=4. Different habitats: agricultural field vs. coastal bay eelgrass.
CONSERVATION PARALLEL! Aleutian Cackling Goose near-extinction and recovery through fox eradication mirrors Trumpeter Swan recovery at 128. Two conservation triumphs within the Anatidae streak.
STREAK EXTENDS! Harlequin (118, E=5, stream) to Cackling (130, E=4, field). Thirteen consecutive Anatidae. From torrential stream to agricultural field.
PAIR PATTERN! Hairy/Downy (110-111, size pair) mirrors Canada/Cackling (129-130, size pair). The fifth comparison pair follows the established pattern.
